narration.default: Last chapter's greet() always said the same thing — "Hello, friend!". What if you wanted to greet Alice, then Bob, then Charlie — each by name? You could define three separate functions, but that's repetitive. The right way is to make ONE function that takes the name as an INPUT — then call it with different names. In Scratch, "Make a Block" let you add input slots to your custom blocks (number, string, boolean) — same idea here. C functions take inputs by listing them in the parentheses; those inputs are called PARAMETERS.

Lines 3 to 5: void greet(char name[]) { printf("Hello, %s!", name); }. The new piece is char name[] inside the parentheses — that's a PARAMETER. char name[] means "a string variable named name". The square brackets [] are part of how C declares string parameters — for now just remember the shape. Inside the function, name acts like a local variable that holds whatever the caller passed in. The %s in the printf is the format code for STRINGS (like %d for int and %c for char). It plugs the name into the message.

Lines 8 and 9: greet("Alice"); and greet("Bob");. Same function, different inputs. The string in the parentheses is the ARGUMENT — the actual value being passed to the function. Inside greet, the parameter name takes on that value (first "Alice", then "Bob"). Notice the strings are in DOUBLE quotes — that's the C convention for strings (single quotes for char). So one function, two different outputs because the caller controls the input.

Click Run. Console: "Hello, Alice!" then "Hello, Bob!". The function is a TEMPLATE; the call fills in the blank. Add a third call: greet("Charlie"); below the first two. Run. Three lines now. You can have hundreds of callers, each with a different argument. This is why functions are so powerful — they make ONE chunk of code reusable in MANY situations.
